conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the eligibility rules so I can guide you through this properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I've reviewed the eligibility rules for Child Benefit. To help determine if you're eligible, I need to ask you some questions.

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you have any children under 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have two children. Alex is 6 years old and Blake is 4 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you for that information. Now I need to understand your living situation with Alex and Blake.

**Do both Alex and Blake currently live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes. Alex lives with me at home, but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I need to understand more about Blake's situation.

**Where does Blake live, and do you make any financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with their other parent. I do contribute a bit towards their upkeep - I help out with things like clothes and some other bits and pieces.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to understand the level of your contributions to determine eligibility for Blake.

**Do your contributions towards Blake's upkeep amount to at least £25.60 per week (which is the current Child Benefit rate for a child who is not the eldest)? This includes clothes, presents, food, pocket money, and any financial contributions towards accommodation.**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, it's not quite that much. I contribute about £20 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have a few more questions to ensure I have a complete picture.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just one final question to complete my assessment:

**Are either Alex or Blake currently in hospital for more than 12 weeks, or in care for more than 8 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in hospital or in care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, here's my determination:

**For Alex (6 years old): ✓ ELIGIBLE**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex because they are under 16, live with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

**For Blake (4 years old): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. While Blake is under 16, they live with their other parent. To qualify for Child Benefit when a child doesn't live with you, you need to contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards their upkeep. Your current contribution of £20 per week is less than the required £25.60 per week threshold.

**What this means:**
- You can claim Child Benefit for Alex
- Blake's other parent (who Blake lives with) would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for Blake

If you wanted to become eligible for Blake in the future, you would need to increase your contributions to at least match the Child Benefit rate, or Blake would need to come and live with you.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 4 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You are responsible for Alex as they live with you. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Alex meets all the criteria for Child Benefit eligibility.

Blake is 4 years old (under 16) but does not live with you - they live with their other parent. According to the rules, you can still be considered responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (for example on food, clothes or pocket money). You contribute approximately £20 per week towards Blake's upkeep, but this is less than the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for a child who is not the eldest. Since your contributions do not meet the minimum threshold required and Blake does not live with you, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake. The other parent would be the person who should claim if anyone does.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (6) and Blake (4). Alex lives with the claimant in the UK, making the claimant eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Blake lives with their other parent, and while the claimant contributes towards Blake's upkeep (£20/week), this falls short of the required Child Benefit amount (£25.60/week). Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex only, not for Blake.

Blake is 4 years old (under 16) but does not live with you - they live with their other parent. You contribute approximately £20 per week towards Blake's upkeep, but this is less than the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for a child who is not the eldest. Since your contributions do not meet the minimum threshold required and Blake does not live with you, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake. The other parent would be the person who should claim if anyone does.

Blake lives with their other parent, and while the claimant contributes towards Blake's upkeep (£20/week), this falls short of the required Child Benefit amount (£25.60/week). Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex only, not for Blake.
